candidate_blocks.section_label: Preparation and characterization of AP-PLGA-NPs AP-PLGA-NPs were prepared by a solvent diffusion meth- odology
candidate_blocks.text_content: Preparation and characterization of AP-PLGA-NPs AP-PLGA-NPs were prepared by a solvent diffusion meth- odology. Brieﬂy, 18 mg of PLGA and 7 mg of AP were dis- solved in 1 ml of acetone to form an organic phase; then the organic phase was poured into 4 ml of stirred aqueous phase (containing 1% polysorbate 80). The formed O/W emulsion was stirred continuously at 800 rpm at 40°C for 12 h with a magnetic stir bar, and the ﬁnal NP suspension was subsequently ﬁltered through a membrane (0.8-μm pore size) to remove the non-incorporated AP.[27,28] The preparation method was optimized with a homo- geneous design combined with response surface methodol- ogy. The PLGA concentration, the volume ratio of the aqueous phase to the oil phase and the AP feeding amount were selected as the factors and were evaluated according to U*12 (1210) homogeneous design. candidate_blocks.text_content: After injection of AP solu- tion, the AUC value of PUE was greatest in the liver, fol- lowed by the kidney, heart, lung, spleen and brain tissues in turn. Compared with AP solution, the distributions of PUE were greatly changed due to the encapsulation of NPs, with increased AUC and prolonged MRT in all tissues and higher Cmax in brain and spleen. Compared with AP solution, the concentrations of AP and PUE in the brain tissue following intravenous administration of AP-PLGA-NPs were signiﬁcantly higher (P < 0.05) at all the time points except for AP concentra- tion at 0.167 h after administration (Figure 6), and the dis- tributions of AP and PUE in the brain showed to be signiﬁcantly different from those of AP solution, with increased AUC, Cmax and prolonged MRT and t1/2. As pre- sented in Tables 2 and 3, after injection of AP-PLGA-NPs, the Te value of PUE in the brain was up to 2.29, which was much higher than that (1.28) of AP solution; further- more, the Re values in the brain were 2.40 and 2.58 for AP and PUE, and the Ce values were 1.91 and 1.89 for AP and PUE, respectively. The results suggested that PLGA-NPs distinctly enhanced the distributions of AP and PUE in brain. candidate_blocks.text_content: 0.29. When treated with AP solution and AP-PLGA- NPs, the neurological deﬁcit scores of MCAO rats signiﬁ- cantly decreased, which were 2.17 ± 0.39 and 1.42 ± 0.51, respectively. In parallel with neurological deﬁcit score, the infarct volume (%) in the IR group was 40.14 ± 2.61%; treatment with AP solution and AP-PLGA-NPs signiﬁcantly reduced the infarct volume by 29.39 ± 3.67% and 20.47 ± 2.26%, respectively. Effects of AP-PLGA-NPs on cerebral histopathology As shown in Figure 8a, no histopathological abnormalities were observed in the ischaemic hippocampus in the sham rats, the neurons kept in eumorphism and were well arranged, the cytoplasm was abundant, and the nuclei were centered in the cell bodies and clearly stained. In contrast, in the IR rats, the hippocampus tissues were damaged seriously in the infarct area, and the neurons were disordered in arrangement and appeared shrunken with eosinophilic cytoplasm and triangulated pycnotic nucleus. However, the number of necrotic neurons in the ischaemic area of the rats treated with AP formulations was markedly decreased, indicating that the ischaemic injury was attenuated after AP treatment. Furthermore, the number of necrotic and condensed neurons was attenu- ated markedly in AP-PLGA-NPs group, compared with that in the AP solution group. Similarly, the number of necrotic and condensed neurons in the ischaemic cortex of the rats treated with AP formula- tions was markedly decreased, and AP-PLGA-NPs produced better neuroprotective effects compared with AP solution (Figure 8b). Effects of AP-PLGA-NPs on apoptosis As can be seen in Figure 9, few TUNEL-positive neurons were found in the sham group; however, there were numer- ous TUNEL-positive cells in the penumbra cortex in MCAO rats 24 h after reperfusion. This mecha- nism was supported by lots of studies in vitro and in vivo.[25,45,51] Previous studies have indicated the protective effects of AP against cerebral ischaemia-reperfusion injury in animals. It can reduce the damage by oxygen-free radicals, increase the activity of antioxidase, decrease the calcium concentration in neurons, inhibit apoptosis of nerve cells and prevent the inﬂammatory response in the ischaemic area in vivo.[8–10] In this study, the neurological deﬁcit scores and infarct volume signiﬁcantly decreased, and the brain inﬁltration of inﬂammatory cells and neuronal pyknosis and karyolysis were signiﬁcantly alleviated 24 h after reperfusion in the rats treated with AP-PLGA-NPs. In addi- tion, AP-PLGA-NPs signiﬁcantly mitigated the apoptosis in nerve cells after cerebral ischaemic injury. candidate_blocks.text_content: brain might also be metabolized to the three metabolites by the some esterase in the brain tissue. In this study, we only determined the concentrations of AP and PUE in blood and brain tissue because the standard substances of penta- acetylpuerarin and tetra-acetylpuerarin were difﬁcult to be obtained. It should be the unchanged AP and the three metabolites that contribute to the brain-protective effects in rats, following intravenous administration of polysorbate 80-coated AP-PLGA-NPs. In a word, compared with the crude drug, AP-PLGA-NPs showed better brain-protective effects due to the sustained release and enhanced perme- ability across the BBB. Conclusions In this study, we successfully prepared and characterized polysorbate 80-coated AP-PLGA-NPs. PLGA-NPs signiﬁ- cantly enhanced the distributions of AP and PUE in the brain in mice, and AP-PLGA-NPs showed better protective effects against cerebral ischaemia-reperfusion injury in rats compared with the crude drug. It can be concluded that polysorbate 80-coated PLGA-NPs can improve the perme- ability of AP cross the BBB and enhance its brain-protective effects in rats.
